# Thesis Bank

## Master thesis

**Virality is old human psychology compressed into algorithmic feedback loops.**

```text
attention → curiosity → comprehension → emotion → social transmission → memory → repetition → trust
```

Modern platforms measure behavioural traces of that chain:

```text
swipe / stay
watch / abandon
finish / replay
save / send
comment / ignore
follow / forget
search / return
```

## Thesis 1: The first frame is the new headline

Old base: AIDA and advertising attention.

Modern translation:

```text
old headline → YouTube thumbnail → Shorts/Reels/TikTok first frame
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Changing the first frame while keeping the body constant will produce major differences in chose-to-view and early retention.
```

Tactics:

- before/after split;
- result first;
- visible mistake;
- proof screenshot;
- face with emotion;
- screen close-up.

## Thesis 2: Information scent beats vague mystery for practical content

Old base: information foraging.

Modern translation:

```text
The viewer asks: Is this for me? Will I get value quickly? How much effort will this take?
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Specific first-frame and hook cues outperform vague “one trick” mystery when the content is educational.
```

## Thesis 3: Text is cognitive load management

Old base: cognitive load, dual coding, multimedia learning, caption research.

Modern translation:

```text
Text helps when it reduces effort. Text hurts when it competes with the visual.
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Short functional text improves retention; large central text over dynamic footage reduces engagement.
```

## Thesis 4: The hook is unresolved tension

Old base: information-gap theory and open-loop psychology.

Modern translation:

```text
A hook creates a specific gap the video must close.
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Open-loop hooks improve completion only when the payoff is concrete, visible, and timely.
```

## Thesis 5: Pattern interruption only works inside a recognisable pattern

Old base: novelty, distinctiveness, von Restorff effect.

Modern translation:

```text
too familiar = ignored
too strange = confusing
familiar + violation = attention
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Category-consistent surprise beats random weirdness.
```

## Thesis 6: Shares are identity acts

Old base: uses and gratifications, social transmission.

Modern translation:

```text
People share content to signal taste, warn a friend, teach someone, express identity, prove a point, or start a conversation.
```

Hypothesis:

```text
“Send this to [specific person]” beats generic “share this” when the recipient is obvious.
```

## Thesis 7: Emotion is the transmission engine

Old base: Berger & Milkman virality research.

Modern translation:

```text
Useful content travels further when it creates active emotion.
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Useful videos with surprise, relief, awe, frustration-with-fix, or identity recognition outperform neutral useful videos on shares.
```

## Thesis 8: Face is an anchor, not the whole ship

Old base: face salience and UGC face-presence research.

Modern translation:

```text
face = trust / emotion / identity
proof = credibility
screen/process = instruction
before-after = transformation
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Early face + proof/process inserts beats full talking-head for educational short-form content.
```

## Thesis 9: Virality is diffusion: algorithmic push + social handoff

Old base: diffusion theory, weak ties, two-step flow.

Modern translation:

```text
algorithmic discovery = platform shows it to strangers
social diffusion = viewers send it to other people
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Videos designed for social handoff have more durable growth than videos designed only for scroll-stop.
```

## Thesis 10: Series beat isolated viral hits

Old base: mere exposure and audience memory.

Modern translation:

```text
one video = useful moment
series = remembered account promise
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Named recurring formats improve follow conversion compared with standalone tips.
```

## Thesis 11: Searchable videos have longer half-life than trend videos

Old base: information seeking.

Modern translation:

```text
trend = feed-native, fast reach, fast decay
search = problem-native, slower reach, longer tail
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Search-aligned videos produce more 7–30 day views than trend-only videos, even if trend videos win at 24h.
```

## Thesis 12: Proof is the new production value

Old base: persuasion and credibility.

Modern translation:

```text
In AI-saturated feeds, proof beats polish.
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Proof-based advice generates more saves and follows than unsupported advice.
```

## Thesis 13: Editing should create semantic change, not sensory noise

Old base: limited-capacity media processing.

Modern translation:

```text
Cuts help when they add information, proof, emotion, or story movement.
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Information-bearing cuts outperform decorative cuts at the same cut frequency.
```

## Thesis 14: The ending shapes remembered value

Old base: peak-end rule.

Modern translation:

```text
The final beat should create resolution, surprise, template value, save reason, send reason, loop, or series promise.
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Videos ending with a clear rule/checklist/before-after reveal get higher saves and rewatch than generic “follow for more” endings.
```

## Thesis 15: Platform grammar is part of the message

Old base: “the medium is the message.”

Modern translation:

```text
TikTok = search + culture + comments
Reels = sendability + identity + Trial Reels
Shorts = chose-to-view + engaged views + search/subscriber conversion
```

Hypothesis:

```text
Platform-native edits outperform identical cross-posts.
```
